metadata.summary: Procurement framework: make-or-buy, SOW, contract type, vendor evaluation, negotiation, ongoing management. Mandatory clause checklist before signing contracts.

content.title: Procurement Management Framework
content.summary: Six-step framework for engaging external vendors: make-or-buy decision, Statement of Work (SOW) with explicit acceptance criteria, contract type selection (Fixed Price / T&M / Cost Plus), weighted vendor evaluation, contract negotiation, and ongoing vendor management. Every contract must pass a mandatory clause checklist before signing.

why.p: Vague SOWs and "lowest price wins" decisions are the primary causes of vendor disputes, cost overruns, and project delays. A structured procurement process forces explicit scope definition, distributes risk appropriately via contract type, and creates an audit trail from requirements through payment. Missing clauses (IP assignment, DPA, termination-for-convenience) create legal exposure that surfaces only during disputes.

list.item: Defining a make-or-buy framework before engaging external vendors
Drafting SOW, Master Services Agreement (MSA), or Data Processing Addendum (DPA) skeletons
Running an RFI / RFP / RFQ process: vendor list, evaluation matrix, weighted scoring
Selecting contract type for a defined scope and risk profile
Vendor risk and security review (SOC 2, ISO 27001, GDPR DPA) intake
Ongoing vendor management: SLA tracking, change requests, performance reviews
Spot purchases under a PO threshold (e.g. under $5K) where formal procurement is overhead
Highly regulated industries with established central procurement and legal teams — defer to org policy
SaaS tools the engineering team can self-onboard with monthly billing (security review still needed, but not a full RFP)

section.name: make-or-buy-decision
section.p: Frame the decision as an explicit comparison including "build internally" or "do nothing" as baseline options.

rule.statement: Always include the "build internally" or "do nothing" option as an explicit row in the evaluation matrix; otherwise procurement becomes a forced choice.
rule.rationale: Without a baseline comparison, the decision optimizes vendor selection rather than whether to engage a vendor at all.

section.p: Factors favouring Make: full control needed, internal expertise sufficient, long-term strategic asset. Factors favouring Buy: faster time-to-capability, specialized expertise needed, risk transfer preferred.

section.name: contract-type-selection
section.p: Match contract structure to scope clarity and risk allocation.

rule.statement: Select contract type based on scope clarity and risk allocation: Fixed Price for well-defined scope (vendor bears cost risk); T&M for unclear scope (buyer bears cost risk); Cost Plus for research or highly uncertain work (buyer bears all cost risk).
rule.rationale: Mismatched contract type is the primary cause of cost overruns and disputes; variable scope on a fixed-price contract guarantees conflict.

rule.statement: For T&M contracts, enforce a hard cap and a weekly burn-rate report; trigger a change request at 70% of cap.
rule.rationale: T&M without a cap produces silent budget overruns; the 70% trigger provides time to re-scope or stop before the cap is hit.

example.title: Contract type selection
example.note: Mobile app development with clear deliverables (design mockups, working app, source code, App Store approval, 95% crash-free): Fixed Price. Cloud infrastructure setup with evolving requirements: T&M with $15K/mo cap and senior/junior rate card.

section.name: sourcing-methods
section.p: Choose RFI, RFP, RFQ, or sole source based on market maturity and scope clarity.

RFI: gather market info before committing to a procurement. RFP: formal proposals with solutions (use for complex work). RFQ: price quotes for defined work (use when scope is clear). Sole source: direct engagement — requires documented justification.

rule.statement: Publish the scoring rubric with weights before proposals arrive. Retroactive weight changes after seeing proposals are bias laundering.
rule.rationale: Pre-published weights force the evaluating team to commit to priorities before they are influenced by vendor positioning.

section.name: mandatory-contract-clause-checklist
section.p: Non-negotiable terms that must be present before execution.

rule.statement: Every contract must include all of the following before signing: IP assignment, sub-processor disclosure, DPA (if PII involved), termination-for-convenience clause with reasonable notice, audit right, SLA with credits, indemnity including AI-output IP, warranty period. Reject any contract missing a clause; do not accept "TBD".
rule.rationale: Missing clauses surface as disputes; an undiscovered IP gap or absent termination clause locks the buyer into a bad vendor indefinitely.

section.name: ongoing-vendor-management
section.p: Payment structures and performance monitoring after contract execution.

rule.statement: Tie payments to acceptance of deliverables, not calendar dates. Default structure: 25% at start, 50% at milestone, 25% at completion for Fixed Price.
rule.rationale: Calendar-based payments reward delivery timing, not delivery quality; milestone-gated payments preserve leverage for final acceptance.

section.name: weighted-scoring
section.p: Structured vendor evaluation with sensitivity analysis.

rule.statement: Run a sensitivity analysis after scoring: re-score with each criterion weight shifted ±10%. If rank order flips, the choice is not robust — re-evaluate or document the trade-off explicitly.
rule.rationale: A rank that changes under small weight variations means the decision is essentially arbitrary within the measured range; false precision hides this.

example.title: Weighted scoring example
example.note: Five criteria: Technical Capability 25%, Relevant Experience 20%, Price 25%, Timeline 15%, Team Quality 10%, References 5%. Vendor A weighted score 86.5, Vendor B 84.5. Sensitivity: swapping price weight to 35% changes winner to Vendor B. Decision documented: technical quality prioritized over cost for this engagement.

section.name: evaluation-anti-patterns
section.p: Common procurement failures and failure modes.

antipattern.description: Lowest price wins without quality and risk weighting.
antipattern.reason: Price-only selection consistently underestimates ramp-up cost, quality gap, and vendor management overhead; total cost of engagement typically exceeds a higher-priced reliable vendor.

antipattern.description: Reference checks summarized from the vendor's own proposal.
antipattern.reason: Self-reported references are marketing; independent reference checks require contacting previous clients directly, not reading testimonials in the bid document.

antipattern.description: Vague SOW accepted to start faster.
antipattern.reason: Every undefined deliverable becomes a dispute; a clear SOW is the single most important document in procurement — spending one week on it saves months of rework.

antipattern.description: No change process defined in the contract.
antipattern.reason: Scope always evolves; without a written change process, every addition is either rejected or silently absorbed until someone notices the cost overrun.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: How to structure procurement tasks for agent orchestration.

The agent is a structured-document generator and an evaluation calculator, not a negotiator. Useful surfaces: (1) draft SOW from a feature spec + acceptance criteria, (2) ingest vendor proposals (PDFs / Markdown) and produce a normalized comparison matrix, (3) compute weighted scores and surface dissents, (4) flag missing clauses against a checklist (IP, DPA, termination, audit, SLA, indemnity, AI-output rights). Every output goes to a human (PM, legal, finance) before sending to vendor.

list.item: A sow-author subagent — emits SOW skeleton from spec + acceptance criteria; validates against clause checklist.
A proposal-normalizer subagent — reads vendor proposals (PDF/MD), outputs canonical JSON: price, timeline, team, references, assumptions.
A vendor-scorer subagent — given normalized proposals + scoring rubric YAML, outputs weighted matrix + sensitivity analysis.
A contract-clause-auditor — diff received contract against checklist; flag missing / weakened clauses.

section.name: sow-author-prompt-pattern
section.p: Template for generating Statement of Work documents.

example.title: SOW generation prompt
example.code:
Inputs: feature_spec.md, acceptance_criteria.md, contract_type_preference="fixed-price", data_classifications=["PII","financial"], jurisdictions=["EU","US"].
Output a SOW with sections: 1. Background / objective (≤ 5 lines). 2. Scope (in / out, bulleted). 3. Deliverables table (id, name, format, due). 4. Acceptance criteria per deliverable. 5. Timeline (milestone, date, payment %). 6. Assumptions and dependencies. 7. Required clauses checklist status: IP assignment, DPA, sub-processor list, audit right, SLA, indemnity (incl. AI), termination for cause, termination for convenience, change process, warranty. For each: [present | missing | weak] with line reference.
Reject and ask for input if any field unspecified. Do NOT invent dollar figures.

section.name: cli-tools
section.p: Command-line utilities and integrations for procurement workflows.

list.item: pandoc — convert SOW Markdown → PDF / DOCX for vendor delivery (https://pandoc.org/)
docusign-cli / dropbox-sign-cli — programmatic e-sign workflows (https://developers.docusign.com/)
gh issue / jira-cli — procurement intake tickets (https://cli.github.com/)
pdfplumber (Python) — extract structured fields from vendor PDFs (https://github.com/jsvine/pdfplumber)
cuelang — validate SOW JSON schema (https://cuelang.org/)
gpg — sign final contracts / NDA exchanges (https://gnupg.org/)
git (procurement-as-code) — version control for SOW / MSA templates (https://git-scm.com/)

section.name: services-and-platforms
section.p: SaaS platforms for procurement automation and vendor management.

list.item: Coupa (SaaS P2P) — enterprise; heavyweight (REST API agent-friendly)
SAP Ariba (SaaS P2P) — enterprise; SAP-aligned (REST API agent-friendly)
Procurify (SaaS) — mid-market (REST API agent-friendly)
Tropic (SaaS) — SaaS-spend negotiation focus (REST API agent-friendly)
Vendr (SaaS) — SaaS procurement assistant (REST API agent-friendly)
Sastrify / Cledara (SaaS) — SaaS cost + contract management (REST API agent-friendly)
Ironclad / LinkSquares / Lexion (SaaS CLM) — contract lifecycle, AI clause review (REST API agent-friendly)
DocuSign / Dropbox Sign / Adobe Sign (SaaS e-sign) — final signature step (REST API agent-friendly)
Vanta / Drata / Whistic (SaaS vendor risk) — security questionnaire automation (REST API agent-friendly)
OneTrust / TrustArc (SaaS privacy) — DPA + sub-processor inventory (REST API agent-friendly)
Notion / Confluence (SaaS) — procurement docs hub (REST API agent-friendly)

section.name: known-limitations
section.p: Where the framework breaks and what to watch for.

list.item: "Lowest price wins" anti-pattern is hard to dislodge once finance owns scoring weights.
SOW without acceptance criteria collapses into endless revisions.
T&M contracts without a cap or burn-rate alert produce silent budget overruns.
IP / data-ownership clauses are hand-waved by both sides until a dispute reveals them.
Termination clauses ("for convenience" with notice + transition) often missing — locks in bad vendors.
Cross-border procurement adds tax, FX, and data-residency complexity that single-jurisdiction templates miss.
AI/ML vendor procurement: training-data rights, model output IP, and indemnity for IP infringement are 2024-2026 hot zones; old templates don't cover them.

template.purpose: Statement of Work with scope, deliverables, acceptance criteria, timeline, assumptions
Weighted evaluation matrix with scoring scale and recommendation sections

script.purpose: Weighted vendor scoring with sensitivity analysis; flags rank instability
